topic: Cursor 3 Parallel Agents Tutorial 2026: Run Multiple AI Agents Simultaneously

key_points: Critical analysis: questions whether Cursor 3 truly introduced parallel agents or just rebranded existing features
Covers technical comparison between Cursor 2.0 and 3.0 parallel agent implementations
Notes that git worktrees were available before Cursor 3 but now have first-class UI support
Discusses /worktree command and Agents Window as key Cursor 3 differentiators
Official documentation for worktrees feature in Cursor
Explains how to select worktree option from agent dropdown
Covers how agents run in isolated git worktrees to prevent conflicts
Details Apply button to merge agent changes back to working branch
Step-by-step guide for running Cursor subagents in parallel using git worktrees
Covers manual git worktree creation commands (git worktree add)
Explains how to scope each agent to its own worktree directory
Practical example with feature branches and PR workflow
Mastering parallel agent mode covering Cursor 2.0 and 3.0
Explains sidebar agent management UI with status indicators
Covers multi-model comparison: running same prompt across multiple models
Tips on pausing/resuming agents to manage system performance
Deep dive into git worktrees as the foundational technology behind parallel agents
Explains how each worktree gets its own filesystem, branch, and agent scope
Covers technical command structure: git worktree add -b feature/x ../feature-x
Best practices: most developers find 2-4 parallel agents manageable, beyond that overhead outweighs benefit

stats.fact: Cursor 3 launched April 2, 2026 with a rebuilt agent-first interface, parallel agents, Design Mode, and the in-house Composer 2 model
Cursor 3 supports up to 8 AI agents running simultaneously across isolated git worktrees
Four parallel agents (UI, API, database, tests) can cut wall-clock development time from 8 hours to 2 hours — a 4x speedup
Building an e-commerce platform took 18 minutes with three parallel agents vs 45+ minutes using traditional sequential workflows
An 8,000-line Next.js migration from pages/ to app/ router completed in roughly 9 minutes using parallel Cloud Agents
Professional developers using AI coding agents report 25-50% productivity gains on average
Early Cursor 3 cloud agent adopters reported spending $2,000+ in just two days due to per-minute VM charges
Cursor pricing tiers: Free, Pro ($20/mo), Pro+ ($60/mo), Ultra ($200/mo) — parallel agents and cloud execution require at minimum the Pro plan
Heavy cloud agent users report monthly overages of 15-30% above their subscription cost; entire annual subscriptions depleted in a single day in documented cases
Cloud Agents bill separately from subscription credits and require MAX mode, which adds a 20% surcharge on every run

angles: Definitive step-by-step Cursor 3 parallel agents tutorial for 2026 — covering all three methods: Agents Window, /multitask command, and manual worktree setup
Git worktrees as the foundational technology: why isolated branches prevent agent conflicts and enable clean PR workflows
Cost-conscious parallel agent strategy: how to get 4x productivity without triggering a $2,000 cloud bill
Real-world full-stack example: UI agent + API agent + database agent + test agent working simultaneously on a single feature
Local vs cloud agents decision framework: when each mode is appropriate based on task type and budget
The /multitask command deep dive: how Cursor automatically decomposes large tasks into parallel subagents
Cursor 3 Agents Window walkthrough: the new sidebar that replaced the traditional IDE-centric workflow
keywords: cursor cloud agents vs local agents
how to run multiple cursor agents at once
